--- SOURCE 2: Global supply chains risks and COVID-19 - ScienceDirect.com ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724

SUMMARY:
After the COVID-19 pandemic, more research is needed to understand how the impacts of global events differ among alternative network structures in the presence of supply chain risks, and how relevant these potential risk mitigation strategies are for Small and Medium Enterprises(SMEs). (2021) argue that more research is needed to understand how the impacts of global events such as COVID-19 pandemic differ according to supply chain network structures in the presence of both supply and demand risks, and how relevant and costly these potential risk mitigation strategies are for generally financially constrained SMEs. It is also important to study how the supply chain configuration facilitates/hinders other possible mitigation strategies mentioned in the literature.

FULL CONTENT:
Global supply chains risks and COVID-19: Supply chain structure as a mitigating strategy for small and medium-sized enterprises - ScienceDirect

Global supply chains risks and COVID-19: Supply chain structure as a mitigating strategy for small and medium-sized enterprises
===============================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Mustafa Cagri Gurbuz a, Oznur Yurt b c, Sena Ozdemir d, Vania Sena e, Wantao Yu f

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusres.2022.113407](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusres.2022.113407 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0148296322008724&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

After the COVID-19 pandemic, more research is needed to understand how the impacts of global events differ among alternative network structures in the presence of [supply chain](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/supply-chain-management "Learn more about supply chain from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") risks, and how relevant these potential risk mitigation strategies are for Small and Medium Enterprises(SMEs). Thus, our main motivation is to show how SMEs can configure their supply chains, and cost-effectively mitigate the risk created by major disruptions. We combined a [case study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/case-study "Learn more about case study from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with a simulation model. The results suggest the greater usefulness of certain network configuration strategies (e.g., collaboration, multi-sourcing) compared to others during catastrophic events. Our results indicate that SMEs can avoid suffering more harm than larger competitors by adopting strategies consisting of an adequate mix of proactive and reactive elements, and that an effective proactive strategy involves building flexibility by increasing the number of geographically spread supply chain partners, allowing for deeper discounts to preserve demand without hurting profits.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322009183)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322009055)

Keywords
--------

Supply chain disruptions

Supply chain risks

Textile industry

Mitigation strategies

COVID-19

SMEs

1. Introduction
---------------

Major supply chain disruptions have been reported by several companies as a result of the recent COVID-19 pandemic ([Ali et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0010), [Ivanov and Dolgui, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0140), [Sharma et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0345)), especially among Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises (SMEs). Indeed, around two-thirds of SMEs’ operations have been significantly affected by disruptions to the supply chain, compared with 40% of larger companies (Manufacturing Growth [Programme, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0235)). However, even among [SMEs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/company "Learn more about SMEs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the impact of the supply chain disruptions on operations has been uneven, as some [industries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") tend to be more exposed to supply chain risks. One such industry is textiles ([Berg et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0025), [ILO (International Labour Organization), 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0130), [Khlystova et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0180)), characterized by very long and deep supply chains dominated by SMEs across a number of countries. The pandemic has obliged these firms to stop production for a number of reasons[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#fn1) ([Berg et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0025), [Lund and Krishnan, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0225)) while at the same time, the cancellation of orders as a result of the falling consumer demand[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#fn2) has led to liquidity shortages among suppliers, causing them difficulties in navigating the crisis ([Amed et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0020); ILO, 2020; [Lund & Krishnan, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0225)).

Unsurprisingly, developing [global supply chain](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/global-supply-chain "Learn more about global supply chain from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") risk mitigation strategies has become a key priority for many companies in the aftermath of the COVID-19 outbreak ([de Sousa Jabbour et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0065), [Ivanov and Dolgui, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0140), [Yu et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0420)).[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#fn3) The supply chain management literature has identified the structure of the supply chain network as an important component in a number of potential strategies for mitigating risks along the supply chain ([Mostafiz et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0270), [Parmigiani et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0295)). However, [Chowdhury et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0050) argue that more research is needed to understand how the impacts of global events such as COVID-19 pandemic differ according to supply chain network structures in the presence of both supply and demand risks, and how relevant and costly these potential risk mitigation strategies are for generally financially constrained SMEs. It is also important to study how the supply chain configuration facilitates/hinders other possible mitigation strategies mentioned in the literature.

Our study aims to answer the following questions to address this research gap in the literature: a) How can SMEs configure the structure of their supply chains to cost-effectively mitigate the risk created by major disruptions along the supply chain? Specifically, to achieve better risk mitigation, is there any justification for the increased operational costs for establishing supply chain configurations with multiple smaller suppliers and buyers, compared to serial supply chains with a single/larger supplier and buyer? b) When major global supply and demand related disruptions impact multiple supply chain entities, what is the additional benefit of the geographical dispersion of suppliers and/or buyers? and c) Are financial offers (e.g., discounts) to prevent order cancellations (demand disruptions) and maintain profitability more effective under particular network configurations, and are smaller discounts sufficient when dealing with multiple SMEs as buyers, as opposed to a single large buyer?

In order to address these research questions and ground our model in the reality of an SME, we first worked with a textile company based in Turkey to better understand the risks following the COVID-19 breakout, as well as to identify a number of mitigation strategies it adopted in its supply chain. This initial work has led to the development of a simulation model, which considers our [case study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/case-study "Learn more about case study from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") firm's two most important mitigation strategies: (1) flexibility in sourcing/sales in supply chains and impact of partnering with multiple smaller firms and (2) financial offers as a reactive strategy to preserve demand. The value of these strategies is assessed under three alternative stylized supply chain configurations across three echelons (i.e., supplier(s) in the upper echelon, followed by the focal firm and buyer(s), respectively). In all these configurations, the intermediary (focal firm/case company) acts as an agent absorbing most of the shocks in the demand and supply processes (i.e., supplier disruption leading to lost sales in lower echelons (i.e., buyers), and demand shocks leading to order cancellations in upper echelons (i.e., suppliers)).

This study contributes to both theory and practice in three main ways. First, during catastrophic events, from a practical perspective, the case study results suggest that there are differences in the value of various network configuration strategies (e.g., collaboration, multi-sourcing, customer diversification) that connect an SME (the focal firm) with other SMEs. This further supports our finding that it is a simplification to state that the general claim that SMEs find it harder to cope with major disruptions because of the lack of financial resources and strong ties with supply chain partners ([OECD, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0285)), and that reality is more complex. Such network configuration strategies allow SMEs to deploy proper risk mitigation strategies as well.

Second, from a theoretical perspective, our simulation model, informed by the findings of the case study, extends the network configurations approach by analyzing how different supply chain types compare in mitigating risks of an environmental influence (such as disruptions occurred during COVID-19 pandemic), which by nature do not necessarily disrupt all diverse network actors simultaneously ([Meyer et al., 1993](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0260), [Kwak et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0200)) and therefore provides insights into the benefits of partnering with reliable firms that function even under catastrophic situations. To the best of our knowledge, our simulation model is quite generic and the first to explicitly consider both major supply and demand disruptions, as well as minor fluctuations in cost/demand parameters during catastrophic events, even when an individual supplier/buyer may not be directly disrupted, accounting for conditional dependencies.

Finally, from a methodological perspective, combining an industrial case study with a simulation model yields a number of meaningful and timely insights for both researchers and practitioners during and post-COVID-19. The simulation model provides a practical tool, enabling firms to perform cost-benefit analysis (especially critical for SMEs) regarding relevant proactive and reactive mitigation strategies for networks subject to demand, supply, and transportation risks.

2. Literature review
--------------------

### 2.1. Supply chain risk and mitigation strategies in the aftermath of COVID-19

Scholarly interest has recently grown in supply chain disruptions and relevant risks (e.g. [Ali et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0015), [Chowdhury et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0055), [Karmaker et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0160), [Yu et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0420)). Mitigation of the supply chain risks, viewed as a critical organizational capability, involves the management of the impact of unexpected network disruptions, as well as the capability to restore the operations ([Ali et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0010), [Ali et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0015), [Raj et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0320), [Tukamuhabwa et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0380), [Yu et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0420)). Several categories and classifications of supply chain risk and risk mitigation strategies have been proposed (e.g. [Christopher et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0045), [Ho et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0125), [Manuj and Mentzer, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0240)). Among these, the risk due to catastrophic events has received some attention (e.g. [Chen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0040), [Meena and Sarmah, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0250), [Paul et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0305), [Scholten et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0350), [Shahed et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0355)). Most work, however, has focused on the sources of relevant disruptions (e.g. [Meena et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0255)) and on the strategies to mitigate relevant risks (e.g. [Kleindorfer & Saad, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0190); Christopher et al., 2020; 2011), rather than the key role of supply chain structure (e.g. [Garvey et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0090))in the mitigation strategy, particularly when, as in the case of the COVID-19 pandemic, the supply chain is hit simultaneously by supply and demand shocks, ([Chowdhury et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0050)).

The structure of the supply chain imposes constraints on how risk mitigation strategies can be designed and deployed. Global disruptions have far-reaching impact on supply chains simultaneously on multiple interrelated dimensions (e.g., production, transportation, demand, finances), which makes the supply chain recovery challenging ([Paul et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0300)). The likelihood and impact of such disruptions heavily depend on the network structure. In our research, we discuss network structure's role in risk mitigation as a proactive strategy, and on the deployment of reactive strategies as a rapid response to supply chain disruptions. As such, we briefly discuss the relevant literature on network configurations, supplier/buyer concentration, geographical concentration, and impact of financial offers in mitigating demand risk in the remainder of Section 2.

### 2.2. Network configuration theory in supply chain disruptions

This study examines network configurations as value-creating systems in which the focal firm configures the structure of its relationships with its suppliers and buyers to achieve a specific strategic goal or outcome, e.g. to mitigate supply chain disruptions ([Corsaro et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0060), [Kim, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0185), [Pittaway et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0310)). Such relationship structures may include triadic or more complex structures, comprising multiple dyadic and tiered relationships ([Kim, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0185)).

Networks may be configured to diversify the customer base in order to cater for changing states in customer orders due to ongoing shifts and disruptions in demand ([Akanle & Zhang, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0005)) and/or to diversify the supplier base in order to address changing supply conditions, such as restrictions, shortages or delays ([Hendricks et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0100), [Kaki et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0155)). Consequently, different network configurations may facilitate different risk mitigation strategies to cope with the joint impact of supply and [demand uncertainties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/demand-uncertainty "Learn more about demand uncertainties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). However, the simultaneous effects of these two types of uncertainty have received only limited attention in the literature (Golmohammadi & Hassini, 2020; [Huang et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0105), [Kazaz, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0165)). Our study contributes to the existing literature analyzing the deployment of network configurations that consider either end of the supply chain to be able to manage severe disruptions affecting multiple actors simultaneously (as opposed to focusing on more predictable or frequent disruptions caused by individual actors: see [Lin and Wang, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0220), [Namdar et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0280))).

Geographical spread of suppliers and customers is another key parameter in network configuration ([Rudberg & Olhager, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0325)), and a critical factor in the transformation of supply chain members' [global supply chains](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/global-supply-chain "Learn more about global supply chains from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the aftermath of the COVID-19 (Ke at al., 2022). The theory developed so far has limited focus on the start point and duration of the period over which environmental influences (e.g., supply chain disruptions) may affect diverse network actors, and generally fails to consider whether or not such influences affect all network actors simultaneously. This is important, because, for instance, the timing of such disruptions and effects on various suppliers in the network partly determines the value of supply base diversification ([Berger and Zeng, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0030), [Burke et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0035), [Lin and Wang, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0220), [Namdar et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0280)). Our study extends configuration theory by specifically considering the external environment of its network actors, in addition to how configurations should be deployed to fit a focal firm’s immediate environment.

### 2.3. Supplier and customer concentration in supply chain disruptions

The level of concentration of the supply chain defines the extent to which a firm depends on suppliers and buyers for business continuity. Supplier concentration indicates the number of suppliers and work distribution among these ([Sako et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0335), [Steven et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0365)). Similarly, down the chain, customer concentration represents the degree of concentration of revenues across customers ([Ke et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0170)), ultimately defining the share of revenues from each ([Saboo et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0330)).

As far as the supply chain is concerned, greater supplier concentration (i.e., smaller number of suppliers as sources) has the following three major benefits: (1) it facilitates the development of stronger and longer term relationships, because a small group is able to meet sourcing criteria such as quality and cost of supplies, speed or timeliness of deliveries ([Burke et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0035), [Trevelen and Schweikhart, 1988](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0375), [Yang and Yang, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0400)); 2) it enables suppliers to become familiar with their buyers’ needs and their requirements via continuous improvement initiatives ([Namdar et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0280)), and (3) it lowers administrative and transaction costs due to reductions in efforts to co-ordinate the supply base, and thus, in negotiating time, and in delays or disturbances in production schedules ([Berger and Zeng, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0030), [Yang and Yang, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0400)). Not all firms, however, source from only one (or few) suppliers, and it is common practice to use multi-sourcing or backup suppliers in a supply chain network. For example, Samsung Electronics Co. ltd. tries to always source from at least two suppliers within its network, even if the share of the second in total order volume is as low as 20% ([Sodhi & Lee, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0360)). The main driver for “reduced supplier concentration” is the ability to limit the dependency on individual suppliers, particularly in disrupted and highly uncertain environments ([Yang & Yang, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0400)). The use of, for instance, backup suppliers, even if costly, is particularly valuable when there is a need to prioritize dedicated supplier reliability ([Kumar et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0195), [Yin and Wang, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0415)), and this approach is crucial in developing flexibility in sourcing.

On the demand side of the equation, greater customer concentration may have more potential in two respects: (1) strengthening the ability of supply chain risk mitigation against demand uncertainty in disruptions, such as COVID-19, due to the increased targeting efforts towards stronger relational ties with fewer customers ([Saboo et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0330), [Wang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0390)), and (2) increasing firm revenues through [economies of scale](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/economies-of-scale "Learn more about economies of scale from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by lowering overhead costs and selling expenses, and facilitating productions and transactions ([Kwak and Kim, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0205), [Wang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296322008724#b0390)). However, despite such attracti

enriched_points.point_title: Two-thirds of SMEs faced significant impacts
enriched_points.expanded_content: Approximately 66% of SMEs reported major supply chain disruptions, affecting inventory management and operational capabilities during the COVID-19 pandemic, highlighting their vulnerability to external shocks.
Even larger firms experienced significant challenges, with 40% reporting disruptions, indicating that no sector was immune to the widespread effects of COVID-19 on supply chains.

Businesses have shifted priorities toward ensuring operational continuity, implementing strategies to adapt to disruptions, and strengthening resilience in supply chain networks for future uncertainties.
The pandemic prompted a critical reassessment of supply chain strategies, urging firms to evaluate risk management practices and embrace innovative approaches to enhance supply chain resilience.
